transcript: Welcome back. You have now built a strong analytics foundation across the last four lessons. You know which metrics matter, you have set up tracking in Go High Level, you can read and interpret campaign reports, and you understand how to use Google Analytics and UTM parameters to trace traffic all the way to its source. Now it is time to use all of that knowledge to actively improve performance. And the most reliable tool for doing that is A-B testing. In this lesson, you are going to learn what a valid A-B test looks like, what to test and when, how to read your results correctly, and how to avoid the mistakes that make most test results completely useless. Let's get into it. Let me start by being direct about something. Most people who claim they are running A-B tests are actually just making changes and hoping things improve. That is not testing. That is optimistic tinkering. A real A-B test is a controlled experiment. You take one thing, one page, one email, one workflow, and you create two versions that are identical except for a single, clearly defined variable. You expose each version to an equal share of your audience under identical conditions. You wait for a statistically significant number of results to accumulate. And then, only then, you declare a winner based on the data. The reason this matters so much is that when you change multiple things at once and results improve, you have no idea which change caused the improvement. You cannot repeat it reliably, you cannot scale it, and you cannot present it to a client with confidence because you genuinely do not know what worked. When you test correctly, every experiment teaches you something permanent. You build a library of proven insights about your audience that compounds over time and makes every future campaign smarter than the last. To make testing consistent and reliable, use this five-step framework on every test you run, regardless of whether you are testing an email, a landing page, or a workflow. Step one is identify the problem. Go to your reports, your GHL dashboard, your campaign statistics, your GA4 acquisition report, and find a metric that is underperforming. An open rate below 20%, a landing page conversion rate below 10%, a workflow completion rate below 60%, a specific step in a funnel where contacts are dropping. Your test must be anchored to a real measurable problem. If you are just testing because it feels like a good idea with no specific metric in mind, you are wasting time. Step two is form a hypothesis. A hypothesis is not a guess. It is a structured prediction. Use this format. If we change X to Y, then metric deed will improve because of reason W. For example, if we change the subject line from a generic announcement format to a question-based format, then the open rate will improve, because questions trigger curiosity and feel more personally relevant. This structure forces you to think through your reasoning before you test, which means you learn something whether the test wins or loses. Step 3 is build version A and version B. Version A is always your current existing version, the control. Version B is the challenger, the one change you are testing. Nothing else changes. The email body, the landing page layout, the workflow timing, everything except your one test variable must be identical across both versions. Step four is run the test with enough traffic This is the step most specialists skip too early Do not stop your test after two days because one version looks better Do not stop after 30 cents per variant Wait until each version has accumulated at least 100 conversions Not cents, not impressions, but conversions. And aim for a confidence level of 95% or higher before drawing any conclusions. Step 5 is analyze and implement. Once you reach statistical significance, declare the winner, implement it as the new standard, and document the result in your testing tracker. That documentation is the asset. It is the institutional knowledge that makes you more valuable to every client over time. Let us now get into the specific elements worth testing. Starting with email campaigns because they offer the fastest feedback loop. There are six high-impact variables in email marketing. The first and most important is the subject line. Subject lines are the gatekeeper to everything else. If nobody opens the email, the rest of your work is invisible. Test emotional versus informational subject lines, short versus long, question formats versus statement formats, and personalized versus generic. Subject line tests typically produce results within 48 to 72 hours on a warm list. The second is preview text, also called the preheader. This is the short line of text that appears next to the subject line in the inbox before someone opens the email. Most specialists never test this, which means it is a low-hanging opportunity. A strong preheader that extends the curiosity of the subject line can lift open rates meaningfully. The third is the sender name. Sending from a person's name rather than a brand name often increases open rates because it feels more like a personal message and less like a broadcast. Test Libre Academy against Maria from Libre Academy, and you will frequently find the personal name version wins. The fourth is the email body opening. The first sentence of your email determines whether someone reads the rest. Test opening with a statistic against opening with a story or a direct question. The fifth is the call to action button. Test the text on the button, the color of the button, and the placement of the button. Download Now vs. Get Instant Access is a classic test that frequently produces a 10-20% difference in click-through rate. The sixth is Send Time and Day. Different audiences have different peak engagement windows. Test Tuesday at 9am against Thursday at 1pm and compare open rates. Over time, you will find the pattern specific to your client's audience. and that data becomes a permanent advantage. Landing page. A-slash-B testing tends to have a longer feedback loop than email testing because you need a consistent stream of traffic to generate enough conversion data. But the payoff is significant. A 3% lift in landing page conversion rate on a high-traffic page can mean hundreds of additional leads per month. The six highest impact landing page variables are the headline, hero image or video, call to action button text, call to action button color, form length, and social proof placement. The headline test should almost always run first. The headline is the first thing a visitor reads, and research consistently shows it has the highest single impact on whether someone stays or bounces. Test a benefit-led headline against a question-based headline. Test a specific outcome headline. For example, get your first automation workflow running in 60 minutes. Against a curiosity headline. Most business owners are losing 30% of their leads. Here's the fix. Form length is worth particular attention for service-based businesses. Reducing a five-field form to a two-field form almost always increases submission rate, but it may also reduce lead quality. The right answer depends on the business sales process A high low business may want more leads and accept lower quality A high coaching business may prefer fewer better submissions Test both and look at lead quality downstream in the pipeline, not just submission count at the top of the funnel. Workflow testing is the area most specialists overlook entirely. but it is where some of the most significant optimization opportunities live. The four most valuable workflow variables to test are email sequence timing, email position in the sequence, channel order in multi-channel workflows, and the wait duration before an if-slash-else condition fires. Timing tests are the most straightforward. Does a one-day wait between emails produce better engagement than a two-day wait? The answer varies significantly between audiences. A high-intent lead who just opted in for a free guide may be ready for a follow-up email within 24 hours. A cold prospect added via a paid ad may need 48 to 72 hours before a follow-up feels welcome rather than aggressive. Channel order tests in multi-channel workflows reveal whether your audience responds better to being led with email or led with SMS. Some audiences engage better with email first and treat SMS as a secondary nudge. Others, particularly mobile-first audiences, engage far more strongly with SMS as the first touchpoint. Test the order and let the engagement data decide. One important note on workflow testing. Because contacts move through a workflow at different speeds depending on wait durations, workflow tests take longer to produce reliable results than email broadcast tests. Allow at minimum 2-3 weeks of data collection before reading results. Reading a slash B test results correctly requires understanding three numbers, sample size, confidence level, and relative lift. Sample size is the most commonly ignored. You need at least 100 conversions, not sends, not opens, but conversions, in each variant before you can trust the result. Below that threshold, the difference between version A and version B could simply be random noise. Many specialists declare a winner after 30 or 40 conversions and implement a change based on data that does not actually mean anything. Confidence level tells you how likely it is that your result reflects a real difference rather than chance. The industry standard is 95% confidence. If your A-B testing tool shows a confidence level below 95%, keep the test running. Most professional email platforms and landing page tools calculate this automatically and display it as a percentage or a simple indicator. Relative lift is how you communicate the result to a client. The formula spoken aloud is relative lift equals version B result minus version A result, then divided by version A result, then multiplied by 100. For example, if version A has a conversion rate of 12% and version B has a conversion rate of 16%, then relative lift equals 16 minus 12, which equals 4, then divided by 12, which equals 0.333, then multiplied by 100, which equals 33.3% improvement. That is the number you present to the client, not we went from 12 to 16%. But version B produced a 33% improvement in conversion rate. Go High Level does not have a native A-B split testing feature for automated workflow emails. But you can conduct manual A-B tests by creating two separate broadcast sends to equally segmented lists, then comparing results in the email statistics panel. This screenshot shows two broadcast statistics side open rate click rate and total sends for each version Use the annotation I have highlighted to locate the fields you need For landing page A slash B testing Go High Level does offer a built split testing feature within the funnel and website builder, which randomly routes incoming traffic between two-page versions and tracks conversions for each. Before we close, let me walk you through the six mistakes that make A slash B test results completely worthless, because I see all of these regularly. The first is testing multiple variables at once. If you change the subject line, the CTA button, and the hero image at the same time and conversions improve, you have no idea which change caused the lift. You cannot reproduce it, you cannot teach it to a client and you cannot build on it. The second is stopping the test too early. A result that looks like a clear winner on day two frequently reverses by day seven as more diverse traffic enters the sample. Always wait for statistical significance before declaring a winner. The third is running an uneven traffic split. Both versions must receive equal traffic. If version A gets 90% of sends and version B gets 10%, your version B sample is too small to generate reliable data. The fourth is testing on too small a list. Running a test on 50 contacts per variant does not give you enough data to distinguish a real pattern from random chance. You need meaningful volume. The fifth is not documenting results. Every completed test is a permanent asset. If you fail to record the hypothesis, the variable tested, the results, and the conclusion, you will repeat the same tests and lose the compounding benefit of accumulated learning. Your A-slash-B test tracker template makes documentation automatic. The sixth is ignoring external factors when reading results. If one of your variants ran during a promotional period, a public holiday, or a major news event that affected your audience's attention and behavior, the results are contaminated. Always note any external context in your test documentation. Let us close with the six key takeaways from this lesson. First, a slash B testing is a controlled experiment, not an optimistic change followed by hopeful observation. Change one variable, split traffic equally, and wait for statistical significance. Second, use the five-step framework on every test. Identify the problem, form a hypothesis, build versions A and B, Run with enough traffic and analyze and implement. Third, in email campaigns, start with subject line tests. They have the highest single impact on open rate and produce results within 48 to 72 hours. Fourth, in landing page tests, start with the headline. It is the highest leveraged single element on any page. Fifth, read results using three numbers. Sample size of at least 100 conversions per variant Confidence level of at least 95% And relative lift expressed as a percentage improvement Sixth, document every test result. Your testing history is one of the most valuable professional assets you will build as a marketing automation specialist. In the next lesson, Lesson 8.6, we are going to cover Conversion Rate Optimization, or CRO. A-slash-B testing is one tool inside CRO, but CRO is the broader system for diagnosing why visitors are not converting and building a structured plan to fix it. You are going to learn the full CRO toolkit. heat maps, scroll maps, friction analysis, funnel audit frameworks, and how to prioritize what to optimize first when you are working with a real client. I will see you there.

visualDescriptions.description:
The image presents a comparison between guessing and A/B testing, highlighting the benefits of using data-driven approaches over relying on opinions.

* **Title**: 
	+ The title is "Opinions Are Free. Data Is Priceless."
	+ It emphasizes the importance of data over opinions.
* **Subtitle**:
	+ The subtitle reads, "The difference between guessing and testing."
	+ It sets the context for the comparison that follows.
* **Guessing Section**:
	+ This section is presented in a pink box with a red icon.
	+ It lists three examples of guessing:
		- "I think a red button will convert better"
		- "My client prefers a longer subject line"
		- "We changed three things at once and results improved"
	+ The result of guessing is described as "No reliable conclusion, no repeatable insight."
* **A/B Testing Section**:
	+ This section is presented in a green box with a green icon.
	+ It lists three key points about A/B testing:
		- "We tested one variable with equal traffic split"
		- "After 200 conversions, Version B won by 34%"
		- "We know exactly what caused the improvement"
	+ The result of A/B testing is described as "Confident decision, scalable insight."
* **Quote**:
	+ A quote at the bottom reads, "A/B testing turns opinions into evidence."

In summary, the image effectively illustrates the difference between making decisions based on guesses versus using A/B testing to inform choices. By presenting specific examples and outcomes, it demonstrates how A/B testing provides more reliable and actionable insights compared to guessing.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to A/B testing, titled "Run Every Test the Same Way, Every Time" and subtitled "The 5-Step A/B Testing Framework." The infographic outlines a structured approach to conducting A/B tests, ensuring reliability and accuracy in the results. Here are the main points:

• **Identify the Problem**
	+ Action: Determine exactly what metric is underperforming.
	+ Source: Use your GHL or GA4 report to find the specific bottleneck.

• **Form a Hypothesis**
	+ Action: State clearly what you will change and why.
	+ Format: "If we change X to Y, then metric Z will improve because of reason W."

• **Build Version A and B**
	+ Action: Change only ONE variable between the two versions.
	+ Rule: Everything else must stay identical to isolate the cause.

• **Run Test with Enough Traffic**
	+ Action: Do not stop early. Let the data accumulate.
	+ Target: Wait for at least 100 conversions or 95% confidence in both variants.

• **Analyze and Implement**
	+ Action: Declare the winner based on statistical significance.
	+ Success: Implement the winner and document the result.

The infographic also includes a warning at the bottom, emphasizing that skipping any step makes the results unreliable and encouraging users to follow the framework every time.

In summary, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide to conducting A/B tests, highlighting the importance of following a structured approach to ensure reliable and accurate results. By following these five steps, users can effectively identify problems, form hypotheses, build test versions, run tests with sufficient traffic, and analyze and implement the results.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to A/B testing, titled "Run Every Test the Same Way, Every Time" and subtitled "The 5-Step A/B Testing Framework." The infographic is divided into five distinct sections, each representing a step in the A/B testing process.

**Step 1: Identify the Problem**
• **Action:** Determine exactly what metric is underperforming.
• **Source:** Use your GHL or GA4 report to find the specific bottleneck.

**Step 2: Form a Hypothesis**
• **Action:** State clearly what you will change and why.
• **Format:** "If we change X to Y, then metric Z will improve because of reason W."

**Step 3: Build Version A and B**
• **Action:** Change only ONE variable between the two versions.
• **Rule:** Everything else must stay identical to isolate the cause.

**Step 4: Run Test with Enough Traffic**
• **Action:** Do not stop early. Let the data accumulate.
• **Target:** Wait for at least 100 conversions or 95% confidence in both variants.

**Step 5: Analyze and Implement**
• **Action:** Declare the winner based on statistical significance.
• **Success:** Implement the winner and document the result.

A warning at the bottom of the image emphasizes that skipping any step makes results unreliable and advises following the framework every time. The infographic provides a clear and structured approach to A/B testing, ensuring that users can conduct tests effectively and accurately.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to email A/B testing, focusing on six high-impact variables that can significantly influence the effectiveness of email campaigns. The title, "Email A/B Testing: 6 High-Impact Variables," sets the stage for an in-depth exploration of these critical elements.

* **Subject Line**
	+ Affects: Open Rate
	+ Version A: "Get your free guide"
	+ Version B: "Your free guide is waiting, [First Name]"
* **Preview Text (Preheader)**
	+ Affects: Open Rate
	+ Version A: "Download now and get started"
	+ Version B: "3 steps to your first automation — inside"
* **Sender Name**
	+ Affects: Open Rate & Trust
	+ Version A: "Libre Academy"
	+ Version B: "Maria from Libre Academy"
* **Email Body Opening**
	+ Affects: Click-Through Rate
	+ Version A: Starting with a statistic
	+ Version B: Starting with a personal story
* **CTA Button**
	+ Affects: Click-Through Rate
	+ Version A: "Download Now"
	+ Version B: "Get Instant Access"
* **Send Time and Day**
	+ Affects: Open Rate
	+ Version A: Tuesday 9 AM
	+ Version B: Thursday 1 PM

**Key Takeaway:**
Test one variable per experiment. Test subject lines first — they have the biggest impact on open rate.

In summary, the image provides a structured approach to email A/B testing, highlighting six crucial variables that can enhance the performance of email campaigns. By understanding and experimenting with these elements, marketers can optimize their emails to improve open rates, click-through rates, and overall campaign success.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to A/B testing on landing pages, focusing on six high-impact variables that can significantly influence user behavior. The title, "Landing Page A/B Testing: 6 High-Impact Variables," sets the stage for an informative exploration of these key elements.

* **Headline**
	+ Affects: Bounce & Submission
	+ Version A: Benefit-led headline
	+ Version B: Question-based headline
* **Hero Image or Video**
	+ Affects: Scroll Depth & Engagement
	+ Version A: Static lifestyle image
	+ Version B: Short explainer video
* **CTA Button Text**
	+ Affects: Submission Rate
	+ Version A: "Submit"
	+ Version B: "Get My Free Guide"
* **CTA Button Color**
	+ Affects: Visual Prominence
	+ Version A: Grey button
	+ Version B: High-contrast brand color
* **Form Length**
	+ Affects: Submission Rate
	+ Version A: 5 fields (name, email, etc.)
	+ Version B: 2 fields (name, email)
* **Social Proof**
	+ Affects: Trust & Conversion
	+ Version A: Testimonials above fold
	+ Version B: Testimonials below CTA

The infographic provides a clear and concise overview of the six high-impact variables that can be tested on landing pages to optimize user engagement and conversion rates. By understanding the impact of each variable, businesses can make informed decisions about their landing page design and content to improve their overall performance. The note at the bottom of the image highlights the importance of starting with headline and CTA button text, as they produce the most reliable lift.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to workflow A/B testing, focusing on four high-impact variables that can significantly influence the effectiveness of email marketing campaigns. The title, "Workflow A/B Testing: 4 High-Impact Variables," sets the stage for an in-depth exploration of these critical factors.

**Variables and Their Descriptions**

*   **Email Sequence Timing**
    *   **What to Test:** 1-day wait vs. 2-day wait between emails
    *   **Measure:** Workflow completion rate and CTR at each step
*   **Email Position in Sequence**
    *   **What to Test:** Move a high-performing email earlier in the sequence
    *   **Measure:** CTR on that specific email and downstream conversion
*   **Channel Order**
    *   **What to Test:** Email then SMS vs. SMS then Email on Day 2
    *   **Measure:** Engagement rate and overall conversion rate
*   **Wait Before If/Else**
    *   **What to Test:** Check the condition at 24 hours vs. 48 hours
    *   **Measure:** YES-branch entry rate (e.g., open rate before check)

**Key Takeaways**

*   Workflow tests run slower due to varying contact speeds; allow more time before reading results.

In summary, this infographic provides a structured approach to optimizing email marketing workflows through A/B testing. By examining and adjusting these four high-impact variables, marketers can refine their strategies to improve engagement, conversion rates, and overall campaign performance.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to determining the validity of a test, focusing on three key metrics: Sample Size, Confidence Level, and Relative Lift. The infographic is structured into three main sections, each addressing one of these crucial elements.

*   **Sample Size**
    *   **Definition:** Each variant should have at least 100 conversions (not just impressions or clicks) before drawing conclusions.
    *   **Warning:** Below 100 conversions, results are statistically unreliable and prone to false positives.
*   **Confidence Level**
    *   **Aim:** Aim for ≥95%. This means there is only a 5% chance the result is due to random luck.
    *   **Pro Tip:** Most A/B testing tools display this automatically. If it says "80% confidence," keep testing.
*   **Relative Lift**
    *   **Formula:** Lift = ((Version B - Version A) ÷ Version A) × 100
    *   **Worked Example:**
        *   Version A: 12% | Version B: 16%
        *   (16 - 12) ÷ 12 = 0.333
        *   0.333 × 100 = 33.3% Lift

In summary, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide for evaluating the validity of a test by emphasizing the importance of sample size, confidence level, and relative lift. By following these guidelines, individuals can ensure that their test results are statistically significant and reliable.

The image presents a comprehensive guide to common mistakes that can render test results worthless, along with practical solutions to rectify these errors. The infographic is divided into two columns, with the left column listing the mistakes and the right column providing the corresponding fixes.

**Mistakes That Make Your Test Results Worthless**

*   **Testing multiple variables at once**
    *   You can't isolate what caused the change.
*   **Stopping the test too early**
    *   Day 2 "winner" often reverses by Day 7.
*   **Running an uneven split**
    *   90/10 splits are unreliable for the minority version.
*   **Testing on too small a list**
    *   50 sends per variant produces noise, not data.
*   **Not documenting results**
    *   Without records, you repeat tests and lose gains.
*   **Ignoring seasonal/external factors**
    *   Sale periods can inflate conversions falsely.

**The Fix**

*   **Change one thing only**
    *   Isolate variables to know exactly what works.
*   **Wait for statistical significance**
    *   Don't call it until confidence hits 95%.
*   **Equal traffic distribution**
    *   Both versions must get equal/near-equal traffic.
*   **Target >100 conversions**
    *   You need volume to get valid results.
*   **Treat every result as an asset**
    *   Log every test to build institutional knowledge.
*   **Check context before declaring**
    *   Don't credit a headline change for a holiday spike.

In summary, the infographic provides a clear and concise guide to avoiding common pitfalls in testing, ensuring that results are reliable and actionable. By following these best practices, individuals can increase the validity and usefulness of their test results.

The image presents a concise summary of key takeaways from a lesson on A/B testing, titled "Key Takeaways" in green text at the top left. The subtitle, "What you learned in this lesson," is written in smaller green text below the title.

**Key Takeaways:**

*   **Isolating Variables:** A valid A/B test isolates exactly ONE variable (like a subject line) while splitting traffic equally to ensure an adequate, unbiased sample size.
*   **The 5-Step Framework:** Follow the rigorous process: Identify Problem → Form Hypothesis → Build A/B Versions → Run to Significance → Analyze & Implement.
*   **High-Impact Variables:** Prioritize testing subject lines for email, headlines and CTA text for landing pages, and timing/order for automation workflows.
*   **The Three Magic Numbers:** Validate every test result by checking Sample Size (≥100 conversions), Confidence Level (≥95%), and calculating Relative Lift.
*   **Avoiding Pitfalls:** Steer clear of multi-variable changes, stopping tests too early, uneven traffic splits, small lists, lack of documentation, and ignoring seasonality.
*   **Scalable Insight:** A/B testing systematically replaces subjective opinions with objective evidence, creating repeatable and scalable business growth.

**Additional Information:**

*   **Module and Lesson:** The image indicates that this is Module 8, Lesson 5 of 9, as shown at the bottom left.
*   **Background and Design:** The background features a light green color with a darker green border around the main text box, which contains the key takeaways. A green banner with three horizontal lines is situated in the top-right corner, accompanied by the word "Libre" in green text.
